reasoning_summary: For run 20260421_110929_bf16_gemm_v1_342b1c5 at 30.168576 ms, the 256x128 pivot probe answered the main question cleanly: registers dropped from 200 to 167, but active warps stayed flat at 16.66% while barrier and short-scoreboard stalls jumped to 8.32% and 6.77%. That means the low-register probe did not produce usable throughput and the search should immediately restore the accepted PTX anchor before trying any more follow-on work. After recovery, the best remaining forward families are PTX-local live-state trimming on the accepted surface and a much narrower 256x128 revisit that targets the new barrier/short-scoreboard wall instead of another raw register-only probe.

family_audit.reason: The current probe regressed by +5.981616 ms, so the highest-value next move is to restore the accepted PTX anchor before any new experiment contaminates the baseline.
The 256x128 probe showed that raw register reduction alone is insufficient; the next forward family should return to the accepted PTX surface and trim live control state there.

This family stays alive only because it proved a real register drop to 167, but it is demoted sharply because throughput collapsed and the new local wall is barrier plus short-scoreboard rather than registers alone.
Barrier evidence is stronger after the 256x128 run, so this family remains a valid follow-up once the accepted PTX surface is restored.
The family remains plausible, but it stays below the broader PTX control-path retune because the next step should restore first and then attack the accepted PTX seam.
The 256x128 regression shifted the local signal away from export arithmetic and toward barrier plus short-scoreboard behavior.
The sibling surface remains an alternate branch, but the immediate need is recovery plus PTX-local diagnosis, not another branch jump.
Grouped-row replay is below the cut line because the 256x128 run already established a larger local failure mode that needs cleanup first.

directions.name: Restore the accepted PTX hot-band anchor and discard the failed 256x128 probe
directions.hypothesis: The 256x128 pivot probe achieved the intended register drop but still ran at 30.168576 ms because barrier and short-scoreboard stalls dominated. The correct next step is to restore the accepted PTX hot-band surface immediately so the loop can continue from a sane baseline instead of iterating on a +5.98 ms regression.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Recovery to the known PTX plateau before any further latency-hiding or barrier experiment is attempted
directions.risk: Low. This is a bounded recovery revert back to the accepted PTX surface, not a new optimization hypothesis.
ranking_notes: This is recommended because the current surface is too far off the accepted base to support another meaningful experiment.
The rich NCU deltas make the restore argument stronger, not weaker: registers improved but throughput and barrier behavior regressed badly.
Treat the restored PTX surface as a recovery checkpoint, not as a new win.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the change starts widening beyond a bounded restore or if it fails to return the hot band to the accepted PTX dispatch path.
